expanded_description: A comprehensive guide to drawing Lewis structures for ionic compounds, showing how electron transfer creates charged ions that attract electrostatically, with proper notation for cations, anions, and crystal lattice representation.

core_concept.principle: Lewis structures for ionic compounds show electron transfer from metal atoms to nonmetal atoms, with cations and anions displayed in brackets with their charges indicated.
core_concept.explanation: Ionic Lewis structures differ from covalent structures because they show complete electron transfer rather than sharing. Metal atoms lose valence electrons (shown as dots being removed) to form cations with no dots; nonmetal atoms gain electrons to form anions with complete octets (8 dots). Each ion is enclosed in square brackets with the charge shown as a superscript outside. The electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions is indicated by placing cations and anions adjacent to each other, representing the crystal lattice structure.

image.alt: Lewis structures showing NaCl, MgO, and CaCl₂ with proper ion notation
image.caption: Ionic Lewis structures: cations show no dots (lost electrons), anions show 8 dots (gained electrons), charges in brackets.

sections.introduction: Ionic Lewis structures use specific notation to represent the electron transfer that creates ions. Understanding this notation is essential for correctly drawing and interpreting ionic compound structures.

key_concept.definition: Ionic Lewis structures show ions in square brackets with charges as superscripts outside; cations have no valence dots (electrons lost), anions have 8 valence dots (complete octet from electrons gained).
key_concept.context: This notation distinguishes ionic compounds from covalent compounds and clearly shows the electron transfer that creates the electrostatic attraction holding the compound together.

principles.statement: Each ion is enclosed in square brackets [ ] with the charge indicated as a superscript outside the bracket; this shows the charge applies to the entire ion.
principles.application: Distinguishes ions from neutral atoms; shows magnitude and sign of charge; essential for balancing ionic compound formulas.
principles.example: Na⁺: [Na]⁺ (no dots, +1 charge); Cl⁻: [:Cl:]⁻ (8 dots, -1 charge); Mg²⁺: [Mg]²⁺ (no dots, +2 charge); O²⁻: [:O:]²⁻ (8 dots, -2 charge).
principles.analogy: Like a name tag: brackets are the 'tag' showing this is an ion; superscript shows how many electrons were lost or gained.

image.alt: Diagram showing proper bracket and charge notation for ions
image.caption: Bracket notation: square brackets enclose ion symbol, charge shown as superscript outside.

principles.statement: Cations show no valence electron dots because all valence electrons were lost to achieve noble gas configuration.
principles.application: Shows complete electron loss; cation has empty valence shell exposing previous noble gas core; explains why cations are smaller than parent atoms.
principles.example: Na atom: ·Na (1 dot) → Na⁺ ion: [Na]⁺ (0 dots). Mg atom: ·Mg· (2 dots) → Mg²⁺ ion: [Mg]²⁺ (0 dots).
principles.analogy: Like emptying a wallet: all money (electrons) removed, wallet (ion) is empty but still exists.

image.alt: Comparison of metal atom and cation Lewis symbols
image.caption: Cation representation: all valence dots removed, showing complete electron loss.

principles.statement: Anions show 8 valence electron dots (complete octet) because electrons were gained to fill the valence shell.
principles.application: Shows complete octet achievement; anion has full valence shell matching next noble gas; explains why anions are larger than parent atoms.
principles.example: Cl atom: :Cl· (7 dots) → Cl⁻ ion: [:Cl:]⁻ (8 dots). O atom: :O· (6 dots) → O²⁻ ion: [:O:]²⁻ (8 dots).
principles.analogy: Like filling a puzzle: all 8 pieces (electrons) now in place, puzzle (valence shell) is complete.

image.alt: Comparison of nonmetal atom and anion Lewis symbols
image.caption: Anion representation: 8 dots shown, representing complete octet from gained electrons.

components.definition: Ionic Lewis structure components include element symbols, electron dots, square brackets, and charge superscripts
components.equation: $\text{Ionic Lewis Structure} = [\text{Element Symbol}]^{\text{Charge}} \text{ with appropriate dots}$
components.interpretation: Cations: [Symbol]ⁿ⁺ with 0 dots; Anions: [:Symbol:]ⁿ⁻ with 8 dots (except H⁻ with 2 dots)

worked_examples.title: Drawing Lewis Structures for Simple Ions
worked_examples.problem: Draw Lewis structures for the following ions: (a) K⁺, (b) Ca²⁺, (c) F⁻, (d) S²⁻. Show all valence electrons and charges correctly.

step_by_step.explanation: Determine the neutral atom's valence electrons from group number.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) K: Group 1 → 1 valence e⁻; (b) Ca: Group 2 → 2 valence e⁻; (c) F: Group 17 → 7 valence e⁻; (d) S: Group 16 → 6 valence e⁻

step_by_step.explanation: Determine electrons lost or gained to achieve octet.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) K: lose 1 e⁻ → K⁺; (b) Ca: lose 2 e⁻ → Ca²⁺; (c) F: gain 1 e⁻ → F⁻; (d) S: gain 2 e⁻ → S²⁻

step_by_step.explanation: Draw Lewis structures with brackets and charges.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) K⁺: [K]⁺ (0 dots); (b) Ca²⁺: [Ca]²⁺ (0 dots); (c) F⁻: [:F:]⁻ (8 dots); (d) S²⁻: [:S:]²⁻ (8 dots)

step_by_step.explanation: Verify noble gas configuration for each ion.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) K⁺: [K]⁺ (lost 1 e⁻, no dots). (b) Ca²⁺: [Ca]²⁺ (lost 2 e⁻, no dots). (c) F⁻: [:F:]⁻ (gained 1 e⁻, 8 dots). (d) S²⁻: [:S:]²⁻ (gained 2 e⁻, 8 dots). Cations show no dots; anions show complete octet with 8 dots.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Proper ionic Lewis structure notation with brackets, charges, and appropriate electron dots

sections.section_title: Drawing Lewis Structures for Binary Ionic Compounds
sections.introduction: Binary ionic compounds contain two elements: a metal cation and a nonmetal anion. Lewis structures for these compounds show the electron transfer and the resulting electrostatic attraction between ions.

key_concept.definition: Binary ionic compound Lewis structures show cation and anion pairs with electron transfer indicated; multiple ions are shown when needed to balance charges.
key_concept.context: The ratio of cations to anions is determined by charge balance; Lewis structures show this ratio explicitly with the appropriate number of each ion.

classifications.value: Compounds where cation and anion have equal but opposite charges (+1/-1, +2/-2, etc.)
classifications.behavior: Single cation transfers electrons to single anion; charges balance without needing multiple ions.

examples.explanation: Na: ·Na (1 valence e⁻) transfers to Cl: :Cl· (7 valence e⁻). Result: [Na]⁺ [:Cl:]⁻. One Na⁺ balances one Cl⁻ (+1 + -1 = 0).

image.alt: Lewis structure for NaCl showing electron transfer
image.caption: NaCl Lewis structure: Na transfers 1 e⁻ to Cl, forming [Na]⁺ [:Cl:]⁻.

examples.explanation: Mg: ·Mg· (2 valence e⁻) transfers to O: :O· (6 valence e⁻). Result: [Mg]²⁺ [:O:]²⁻. One Mg²⁺ balances one O²⁻ (+2 + -2 = 0).

image.alt: Lewis structure for MgO showing electron transfer
image.caption: MgO Lewis structure: Mg transfers 2 e⁻ to O, forming [Mg]²⁺ [:O:]²⁻.

examples.explanation: K: ·K (1 valence e⁻) transfers to Br: :Br· (7 valence e⁻). Result: [K]⁺ [:Br:]⁻. One K⁺ balances one Br⁻.

image.alt: Lewis structure for KBr showing electron transfer
image.caption: KBr Lewis structure: K transfers 1 e⁻ to Br, forming [K]⁺ [:Br:]⁻.

classifications.value: Compounds where one cation balances two anions (+2/-1, +4/-2, etc.)
classifications.behavior: One metal atom transfers electrons to two nonmetal atoms; two anions needed to balance one cation's charge.

examples.explanation: Mg: ·Mg· (2 valence e⁻) transfers 1 e⁻ to each of 2 Cl atoms. Result: [Mg]²⁺ [:Cl:]⁻ [:Cl:]⁻. One Mg²⁺ (+2) balances two Cl⁻ (2 × -1 = -2).

image.alt: Lewis structure for MgCl₂ showing electron transfer to two Cl atoms
image.caption: MgCl₂ Lewis structure: Mg transfers 1 e⁻ to each of 2 Cl atoms, forming [Mg]²⁺ 2[:Cl:]⁻.

examples.explanation: Ca: ·Ca· (2 valence e⁻) transfers 1 e⁻ to each of 2 F atoms. Result: [Ca]²⁺ [:F:]⁻ [:F:]⁻. One Ca²⁺ balances two F⁻.

image.alt: Lewis structure for CaF₂ showing electron transfer
image.caption: CaF₂ Lewis structure: Ca transfers 1 e⁻ to each of 2 F atoms.

classifications.value: Compounds where two cations balance one anion (+1/-2, +2/-4, etc.)
classifications.behavior: Two metal atoms each transfer electrons to one nonmetal atom; two cations needed to balance one anion's charge.

examples.explanation: 2 Na atoms: each ·Na (1 valence e⁻) transfer to O: :O· (6 valence e⁻). Result: [Na]⁺ [Na]⁺ [:O:]²⁻. Two Na⁺ (2 × +1 = +2) balance one O²⁻ (-2).

image.alt: Lewis structure for Na₂O showing two Na transferring to one O
image.caption: Na₂O Lewis structure: 2 Na each transfer 1 e⁻ to O, forming 2[Na]⁺ [:O:]²⁻.

examples.explanation: 2 K atoms: each ·K (1 valence e⁻) transfer to S: :S· (6 valence e⁻). Result: [K]⁺ [K]⁺ [:S:]²⁻. Two K⁺ balance one S²⁻.

image.alt: Lewis structure for K₂S showing electron transfer
image.caption: K₂S Lewis structure: 2 K each transfer 1 e⁻ to S, forming 2[K]⁺ [:S:]²⁻.

classifications.value: Compounds where two cations balance three anions (+3/-2, etc.)
characteristics: Total cation charge = total anion charge
classifications.behavior: Two metal atoms transfer electrons to three nonmetal atoms; charge balance requires specific ratio.

examples.explanation: 2 Al atoms: each ·Al· (3 valence e⁻) transfer to 3 O atoms: each :O· (6 valence e⁻). Result: [Al]³⁺ [Al]³⁺ [:O:]²⁻ [:O:]²⁻ [:O:]²⁻. Two Al³⁺ (2 × +3 = +6) balance three O²⁻ (3 × -2 = -6).

image.alt: Lewis structure for Al₂O₃ showing charge balance
image.caption: Al₂O₃ Lewis structure: 2 Al transfer 3 e⁻ each to 3 O atoms, forming 2[Al]³⁺ 3[:O:]²⁻.

principles.statement: The total positive charge from cations must equal the total negative charge from anions; Lewis structures show the correct ion ratio to achieve neutrality.
principles.application: Determines how many of each ion to draw; ensures compound is electrically neutral; predicts chemical formulas from Lewis structures.
principles.analogy: Like balancing a checkbook: credits (positive charges) must equal debits (negative charges) for zero balance.

image.alt: Diagram showing charge balance in ionic compounds
image.caption: Charge balance: total positive charge must equal total negative charge for neutral compound.

principles.statement: Arrows can be drawn from metal valence electrons to nonmetal atoms to show the direction of electron transfer during ion formation.
principles.application: Visualizes the bonding process; shows which electrons move and where they go; helps understand ion formation mechanism.
principles.example: NaCl: arrow from Na's single dot to Cl's unpaired position. MgCl₂: two arrows from Mg's two dots to two Cl atoms (one arrow per Cl).
principles.analogy: Like a delivery arrow: shows where the package (electron) is going from sender (metal) to receiver (nonmetal).

image.alt: Lewis structures with arrows showing electron transfer
image.caption: Electron transfer arrows: show direction of electron movement from metal to nonmetal.

worked_examples.title: Drawing Lewis Structure for MgCl₂
worked_examples.problem: Draw the complete Lewis structure for magnesium chloride (MgCl₂), showing electron transfer, all ions with proper notation, and charge balance.

step_by_step.explanation: Draw Lewis symbols for neutral atoms.
step_by_step.calculation: Mg: ·Mg· (2 valence e⁻); Cl: :Cl· (7 valence e⁻) × 2 atoms

step_by_step.explanation: Show electron transfer with arrows.
step_by_step.calculation: Draw arrow from each Mg valence electron to each Cl atom's unpaired position

step_by_step.explanation: Draw resulting ions with brackets and charges.
step_by_step.calculation: Mg²⁺: [Mg]²⁺ (0 dots); Cl⁻: [:Cl:]⁻ (8 dots) × 2 ions

step_by_step.explanation: Arrange ions to show compound and verify charge balance.
step_by_step.calculation: [Mg]²⁺ [:Cl:]⁻ [:Cl:]⁻; Total: +2 + 2(-1) = 0 ✓ (neutral compound)

worked_examples.final_answer: MgCl₂ Lewis structure: [Mg]²⁺ [:Cl:]⁻ [:Cl:]⁻. Mg loses 2 electrons (one to each Cl), forming Mg²⁺ cation. Each Cl gains 1 electron, forming Cl⁻ anions. Two Cl⁻ ions balance one Mg²⁺ ion for electrical neutrality. All ions shown in brackets with appropriate charges.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Complete ionic Lewis structure drawing with electron transfer, proper notation, and charge balance

worked_examples.title: Drawing Lewis Structure for Al₂O₃
worked_examples.problem: Draw the Lewis structure for aluminum oxide (Al₂O₃), showing the correct ion ratio and charge balance.

step_by_step.explanation: Determine valence electrons for each element.
step_by_step.calculation: Al: Group 13 → 3 valence e⁻; O: Group 16 → 6 valence e⁻

step_by_step.explanation: Determine ion charges from electron transfer.
step_by_step.calculation: Al loses 3 e⁻ → Al³⁺; O gains 2 e⁻ → O²⁻

step_by_step.explanation: Determine ion ratio for charge balance.
Draw complete Lewis structure.

worked_examples.final_answer: Al₂O₃ Lewis structure: 2[Al]³⁺ 3[:O:]²⁻. Two Al atoms each lose 3 electrons (6 total); three O atoms each gain 2 electrons (6 total). Two Al³⁺ cations balance three O²⁻ anions for electrical neutrality. All ions shown in brackets with appropriate charges and electron dots.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Complex ionic Lewis structure with multiple ions of each type and charge balance verification

sections.section_title: Lewis Structures for Ionic Compounds with Polyatomic Ions
sections.introduction: Many ionic compounds contain polyatomic ions—charged groups of covalently bonded atoms. Drawing Lewis structures for these compounds requires showing both the internal covalent bonding within polyatomic ions and the ionic bonding between ions.

key_concept.definition: Polyatomic ion Lewis structures show covalent bonds within the ion (lines between atoms) and the overall ion charge; ionic compounds with polyatomic ions show electrostatic attraction between the polyatomic ion and counter-ions.
key_concept.context: Polyatomic ions behave as single units in ionic bonding; their internal structure is covalent but the ion as a whole participates in ionic bonding with opposite charges.

classifications.value: Positively charged polyatomic ions that form ionic bonds with anions
characteristics: Acts as single unit in ionic bonding
classifications.behavior: N shares electrons with 4 H atoms covalently; overall ion has +1 charge from losing one electron; bonds ionically with anions.

examples.explanation: N (5 valence e⁻) bonds with 4 H (1 valence e⁻ each). N shares 4 e⁻ pairs with H. Total: 5 + 4 - 1 (charge) = 8 e⁻. Structure: [H-N-H]⁺ with 4 N-H bonds, no lone pairs on N.

image.alt: Lewis structure for ammonium ion showing covalent bonds and +1 charge
image.caption: NH₄⁺ Lewis structure: N covalently bonded to 4 H, overall +1 charge in brackets.

examples.explanation: O (6 valence e⁻) bonds with 3 H (1 valence e⁻ each). O has 3 bonds + 1 lone pair. Total: 6 + 3 - 1 (charge) = 8 e⁻. Structure: [H-O-H]⁺ with +1 charge.

image.alt: Lewis structure for hydronium ion
image.caption: H₃O⁺ Lewis structure: O covalently bonded to 3 H, 1 lone pair, overall +1 charge.

classifications.value: Negatively charged polyatomic ions containing oxygen (oxyanions)
characteristics: Central atom bonded to O atoms
classifications.behavior: Central atom (N, S, P, C, etc.) covalently bonded to O atoms; extra electrons create negative charge; resonance distributes charge.

examples.explanation: N (5 valence e⁻) + 3 O (6 e⁻ each) + 1 e⁻ (charge) = 24 e⁻ total. N central, bonded to 3 O. One N=O double bond, two N-O single bonds. Resonance distributes double bond. Overall -1 charge.

image.alt: Lewis structure for nitrate ion showing resonance
image.caption: NO₃⁻ Lewis structure: N central with 3 O, resonance structures, overall -1 charge.

examples.explanation: S (6 valence e⁻) + 4 O (6 e⁻ each) + 2 e⁻ (charge) = 32 e⁻ total. S central, bonded to 4 O. Two S=O double bonds, two S-O single bonds (expanded octet). Overall -2 charge.

image.alt: Lewis structure for sulfate ion
image.caption: SO₄²⁻ Lewis structure: S central with 4 O, expanded octet, overall -2 charge.

examples.explanation: C (4 valence e⁻) + 3 O (6 e⁻ each) + 2 e⁻ (charge) = 24 e⁻ total. C central, bonded to 3 O. One C=O double bond, two C-O single bonds. Resonance. Overall -2 charge.

image.alt: Lewis structure for carbonate ion showing resonance
image.caption: CO₃²⁻ Lewis structure: C central with 3 O, resonance, overall -2 charge.

classifications.value: Negatively charged polyatomic ions without oxygen or with other structures
classifications.behavior: Covalent bonding within ion; overall negative charge from extra electrons; ionic bonding with cations.

examples.explanation: O (6 valence e⁻) + H (1 valence e⁻) + 1 e⁻ (charge) = 8 e⁻ total. O-H single bond, 3 lone pairs on O. Overall -1 charge. Structure: [:O-H]⁻.

image.alt: Lewis structure for hydroxide ion
image.caption: OH⁻ Lewis structure: O-H single bond, 3 lone pairs on O, overall -1 charge.

examples.explanation: C (4 valence e⁻) + N (5 valence e⁻) + 1 e⁻ (charge) = 10 e⁻ total. C≡N triple bond, lone pair on C, lone pair on N. Overall -1 charge. Structure: [:C≡N:]⁻.

image.alt: Lewis structure for cyanide ion
image.caption: CN⁻ Lewis structure: C≡N triple bond, overall -1 charge.

examples.explanation: P (5 valence e⁻) + 4 O (6 e⁻ each) + 3 e⁻ (charge) = 32 e⁻ total. P central, bonded to 4 O. One P=O, three P-O. Expanded octet. Overall -3 charge.

image.alt: Lewis structure for phosphate ion
image.caption: PO₄³⁻ Lewis structure: P central with 4 O, expanded octet, overall -3 charge.

principles.statement: Within polyatomic ions, atoms are held together by covalent bonds (shown as lines); the ion as a whole has a charge from gaining or losing electrons.
principles.application: Distinguish covalent bonds within ion (lines) from ionic bonds between ions (electrostatic attraction); draw complete Lewis structure for polyatomic ion before combining with counter-ions.
principles.example: NH₄⁺: N-H bonds are covalent (lines); NH₄⁺ bonds ionically with Cl⁻. SO₄²⁻: S-O bonds are covalent; SO₄²⁻ bonds ionically with Na⁺.
principles.analogy: Like a sports team: players hold hands (covalent bonds within ion); team competes against other teams (ionic bonds between ions).

image.alt: Diagram showing covalent bonds within polyatomic ion
image.caption: Polyatomic ion structure: covalent bonds (lines) within ion, ionic bonds between ions.

principles.statement: When more than one polyatomic ion is needed in a compound, the ion formula is enclosed in parentheses with subscript indicating the number.
principles.application: Shows multiple polyatomic ions clearly; distinguishes from subscripts within the ion; essential for correct formula writing.
principles.example: Ca(NO₃)₂: one Ca²⁺ + two NO₃⁻ ions. Parentheses show 2 nitrate ions, not 2 O atoms. Mg(OH)₂: one Mg²⁺ + two OH⁻ ions.
principles.analogy: Like ordering meals: (burger + fries) × 2 means 2 complete meals, not 2 burgers and 1 fries.

image.alt: Examples showing parentheses use with polyatomic ions
image.caption: Parentheses usage: (NO₃)₂ means 2 nitrate ions, not 2 oxygen atoms.

principles.statement: Many polyatomic ions have resonance structures where double bonds can be in multiple positions; actual structure is a hybrid of all resonance forms.
principles.application: Explains equal bond lengths in ions like NO₃⁻ and CO₃²⁻; shows charge delocalization; important for understanding ion stability.
principles.example: NO₃⁻: 3 resonance structures with double bond in different positions; actual structure has 3 identical N-O bonds intermediate between single and double.

image.alt: Resonance structures for nitrate ion
image.caption: Resonance in polyatomic ions: multiple valid structures, actual structure is hybrid.

worked_examples.title: Drawing Lewis Structure for Ca(NO₃)₂
worked_examples.problem: Draw the complete Lewis structure for calcium nitrate, Ca(NO₃)₂, showing the polyatomic nitrate ions and their ionic bonding with calcium.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the ions in the compound.
step_by_step.calculation: Ca²⁺ cation + 2 NO₃⁻ anions (parentheses show 2 nitrate ions)

step_by_step.explanation: Draw Lewis structure for Ca²⁺ cation.
step_by_step.calculation: [Ca]²⁺ (no dots, lost 2 valence electrons)

step_by_step.explanation: Draw Lewis structure for NO₃⁻ polyatomic ion.
step_by_step.calculation: N central, 3 O atoms, 24 valence e⁻ total (5 + 18 + 1). One N=O double bond, two N-O single bonds. Resonance. Overall -1 charge in brackets.

step_by_step.explanation: Combine ions showing ionic bonding.

worked_examples.final_answer: Ca(NO₃)₂ Lewis structure: [Ca]²⁺ with 2 NO₃⁻ ions. Each NO₃⁻ has N central with 3 O atoms (one double bond, two single bonds, resonance). Calcium ionically bonded to both nitrate ions. Parentheses in formula indicate 2 nitrate ions per calcium ion.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Lewis structures for ionic compounds with polyatomic ions showing both covalent and ionic bonding

worked_examples.title: Drawing Lewis Structure for Na₂SO₄
worked_examples.problem: Draw the Lewis structure for sodium sulfate, Na₂SO₄, showing the sulfate polyatomic ion and its bonding with sodium ions.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the ions in the compound.
step_by_step.calculation: 2 Na⁺ cations + 1 SO₄²⁻ anion

step_by_step.explanation: Draw Lewis structure for Na⁺ cations.
step_by_step.calculation: [Na]⁺ (no dots) × 2 ions

step_by_step.explanation: Draw Lewis structure for SO₄²⁻ polyatomic ion.
step_by_step.calculation: S central, 4 O atoms, 32 valence e⁻ total (6 + 24 + 2). S has expanded octet with 2 S=O double bonds, 2 S-O single bonds. Overall -2 charge in brackets.

step_by_step.explanation: Combine ions showing ionic bonding.
step_by_step.calculation: [Na]⁺ [Na]⁺ [:O-S(=O)₂-O:]²⁻; Verify charge: 2(+1) + (-2) = 0 ✓

worked_examples.final_answer: Na₂SO₄ Lewis structure: 2[Na]⁺ with 1 SO₄²⁻ ion. Sulfate has S central with 4 O atoms (2 double bonds, 2 single bonds, expanded octet). Two sodium ions ionically bonded to sulfate ion. Charge balance: 2(+1) + (-2) = 0.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Lewis structures for ionic compounds with polyatomic anions and multiple cations

sections.section_title: Crystal Lattice Representation in Lewis Structures
sections.introduction: While Lewis structures show individual ion pairs, ionic compounds actually exist as three-dimensional crystal lattices with many ions. Understanding how Lewis structures relate to crystal lattice structure provides a more complete picture of ionic compounds.

key_concept.definition: Lewis structures show the simplest ion ratio in ionic compounds; crystal lattices extend this ratio in three dimensions with each ion surrounded by multiple oppositely charged ions.
key_concept.context: The Lewis structure represents the empirical formula ratio; the actual crystal contains many repeating units of this ratio in a regular 3D arrangement.

principles.statement: Lewis structures for ionic compounds show the empirical formula (simplest whole-number ratio), not the actual number of ions in the crystal.
principles.application: NaCl Lewis structure shows 1 Na⁺ and 1 Cl⁻, but actual crystal has billions of each in 1:1 ratio; formula represents ratio, not count.
principles.example: NaCl: Lewis shows [Na]⁺ [:Cl:]⁻ (1:1 ratio); crystal has ~10²³ ion pairs. MgCl₂: Lewis shows [Mg]²⁺ 2[:Cl:]⁻ (1:2 ratio); crystal maintains this ratio throughout.
principles.analogy: Like a recipe ratio: 1 cup flour : 2 cups sugar doesn't mean only 3 cups total, just that ratio is maintained regardless of batch size.

image.alt: Comparison of Lewis structure ratio to crystal lattice
image.caption: Lewis structures show empirical formula ratio, not actual ion count in crystal.

principles.statement: In ionic crystal lattices, each ion is surrounded by multiple oppositely charged ions (coordination number); Lewis structures don't show this 3D arrangement.
principles.application: Explains physical properties like high melting points (many attractions to overcome); Lewis structure is simplified 2D representation of 3D lattice.
principles.example: NaCl crystal: each Na⁺ surrounded by 6 Cl⁻; each Cl⁻ surrounded by 6 Na⁺ (coordination number 6). Lewis structure shows 1:1 but not 6:6 coordination.

image.alt: NaCl crystal lattice showing 6:6 coordination
image.caption: Crystal coordination: each ion surrounded by multiple oppositely charged ions in 3D lattice.

principles.statement: Lattice energy (energy released when gaseous ions form solid crystal) is not shown in Lewis structures but is crucial for compound stability.
principles.application: Explains why ionic compounds form despite ionization energy cost; higher lattice energy = more stable compound; relates to ion charges and sizes.
principles.example: NaCl: Lewis shows [Na]⁺ [:Cl:]⁻; lattice energy = -787 kJ/mol (not shown). MgO: Lewis shows [Mg]²⁺ [:O:]²⁻; lattice energy = -3795 kJ/mol (much higher due to +2/-2 charges).
principles.analogy: Like a building: Lewis structure shows the bricks (ions); lattice energy is the mortar holding them together (not visible but essential).

image.alt: Diagram relating Lewis structures to lattice energy
image.caption: Lattice energy: not shown in Lewis structures but crucial for ionic compound stability.

worked_examples.title: Relating Lewis Structure to Crystal Lattice
worked_examples.problem: The Lewis structure for NaCl shows one Na⁺ and one Cl⁻. Explain how this relates to the actual NaCl crystal structure with billions of ions.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify what the Lewis structure represents.
step_by_step.calculation: Lewis structure shows empirical formula ratio (1 Na⁺ : 1 Cl⁻), not actual ion count

step_by_step.explanation: Describe the actual crystal structure.
step_by_step.calculation: NaCl crystal: face-centered cubic lattice with ~10²³ Na⁺ and ~10²³ Cl⁻ in 1:1 ratio

step_by_step.explanation: Explain coordination in the crystal.
step_by_step.calculation: Each Na⁺ surrounded by 6 Cl⁻; each Cl⁻ surrounded by 6 Na⁺ (coordination number 6:6)

step_by_step.explanation: Relate Lewis structure to crystal properties.
step_by_step.calculation: Lewis shows charge balance (+1 + -1 = 0); crystal extends this balance in 3D with many ion-ion attractions (high lattice energy)

worked_examples.final_answer: NaCl Lewis structure [Na]⁺ [:Cl:]⁻ shows the 1:1 empirical formula ratio, not the actual ion count. The crystal contains ~10²³ ion pairs in a face-centered cubic lattice where each Na⁺ is surrounded by 6 Cl⁻ and each Cl⁻ by 6 Na⁺. The Lewis structure represents the fundamental charge balance that repeats throughout the 3D crystal lattice, with lattice energy (-787 kJ/mol) holding the structure together.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Understanding the relationship between simplified Lewis structures and actual 3D crystal lattice structures

sections.section_title: Common Errors and How to Avoid Them
sections.introduction: Students often make specific errors when drawing Lewis structures for ionic compounds. Recognizing and avoiding these common mistakes ensures accurate representations of ionic bonding.

key_concept.definition: Common errors in ionic Lewis structures include: missing brackets, incorrect charge placement, wrong electron counts, confusing ionic and covalent bonding, and incorrect ion ratios.
key_concept.context: Understanding these common errors and their corrections helps develop accurate Lewis structure drawing skills and deeper understanding of ionic bonding.

classifications.value: Forgetting to enclose ions in square brackets or placing brackets incorrectly
characteristics: Brackets around entire compound instead of individual ions
classifications.behavior: Makes it unclear which species is the ion; charge placement ambiguity; doesn't follow standard notation.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: Na⁺ Cl⁻ (no brackets). Correct: [Na]⁺ [:Cl:]⁻ (brackets around each ion). Brackets show charge applies to entire ion.

image.alt: Comparison of correct and incorrect bracket usage
image.caption: Bracket error: ions must be enclosed in brackets with charge outside.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: [Na⁺]. Correct: [Na]⁺. Charge must be outside brackets to show it applies to the entire ion.

image.alt: Correct and incorrect charge placement
image.caption: Charge placement error: charge must be superscript outside brackets.

classifications.value: Wrong number of dots on cations or anions
classifications.behavior: Misrepresents electron transfer; doesn't show noble gas configuration achievement; confuses ionic with covalent bonding.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: [Na·]⁺ (cation showing dot). Correct: [Na]⁺ (no dots). Cations lose all valence electrons, should show no dots.

image.alt: Correct and incorrect cation electron representation
image.caption: Cation error: cations should have no valence dots (all electrons lost).

examples.explanation: Incorrect: [:Cl·]⁻ (7 dots). Correct: [:Cl:]⁻ (8 dots). Anions gain electrons to complete octet, must show 8 dots.

image.alt: Correct and incorrect anion electron representation
image.caption: Anion error: anions must show complete octet (8 dots).

classifications.value: Wrong number of cations or anions shown for charge balance
classifications.behavior: Compound appears charged (impossible); doesn't represent actual compound formula; violates electrical neutrality.

examples.process: Error: MgCl shown instead of MgCl₂
examples.explanation: Incorrect: [Mg]²⁺ [:Cl:]⁻ (charges: +2 + -1 = +1, not neutral). Correct: [Mg]²⁺ [:Cl:]⁻ [:Cl:]⁻ (charges: +2 + 2(-1) = 0).

image.alt: Correct and incorrect ion ratios
image.caption: Ion ratio error: must show correct number of ions for charge balance.

examples.process: Error: NaO shown instead of Na₂O
examples.explanation: Incorrect: [Na]⁺ [:O:]²⁻ (charges: +1 + -2 = -1, not neutral). Correct: [Na]⁺ [Na]⁺ [:O:]²⁻ (charges: 2(+1) + -2 = 0).

image.alt: Correct Na₂O structure showing 2 Na⁺ ions
image.caption: Ion ratio error: Na₂O requires 2 Na⁺ to balance 1 O²⁻.

classifications.value: Drawing covalent bonds (lines) between ions instead of showing electrostatic attraction
classifications.behavior: Misrepresents bonding type; ionic bonds are electrostatic, not shared electrons; changes compound properties prediction.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: Na-Cl (line suggests covalent bond). Correct: [Na]⁺ [:Cl:]⁻ (no line, electrostatic attraction). Ionic bonds don't share electrons.

image.alt: Comparison of ionic vs. covalent representation
image.caption: Bonding error: ionic compounds show electrostatic attraction, not covalent bonds (lines).

principles.statement: Always verify ionic Lewis structures by checking: brackets around each ion, charges outside brackets, cations have no dots, anions have 8 dots, total charge = 0.
principles.application: Systematic verification prevents common errors; ensures accurate representation; builds good drawing habits.
principles.example: MgCl₂ check: Brackets? ✓ [Mg]²⁺ [:Cl:]⁻ [:Cl:]⁻. Charges outside? ✓. Cation dots? 0 ✓. Anion dots? 8 each ✓. Total charge? +2 + 2(-1) = 0 ✓.
principles.analogy: Like a pre-flight checklist: verify each item before considering the structure complete and correct.

worked_examples.title: Identifying and Correcting Lewis Structure Errors
worked_examples.problem: Identify the errors in this Lewis structure: Na⁺ [:Cl:] (no brackets on Na, no charge on Cl). Draw the correct structure for NaCl.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify all errors in the given structure.
step_by_step.calculation: Error 1: Na⁺ without brackets. Error 2: Cl without charge. Error 3: Need to verify electron counts.

step_by_step.explanation: Correct the cation representation.
step_by_step.calculation: Na⁺ → [Na]⁺ (add brackets, keep charge outside)

step_by_step.explanation: Correct the anion representation.
step_by_step.calculation: [:Cl:] → [:Cl:]⁻ (add charge, verify 8 dots)

step_by_step.explanation: Verify the complete corrected structure.
step_by_step.calculation: [Na]⁺ [:Cl:]⁻; Check: brackets ✓, charges outside ✓, cation 0 dots ✓, anion 8 dots ✓, total charge 0 ✓

worked_examples.final_answer: Errors identified: (1) Na⁺ missing brackets, (2) Cl missing charge. Correct structure: [Na]⁺ [:Cl:]⁻. Verification: brackets around each ion ✓, charges outside brackets ✓, Na⁺ has 0 dots ✓, Cl⁻ has 8 dots ✓, total charge = +1 + (-1) = 0 ✓.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Error identification and correction in ionic Lewis structures with systematic verification

summary: Lewis structures for ionic compounds show electron transfer from metals to nonmetals, with ions in square brackets and charges as superscripts outside. Cations: [Symbol]ⁿ⁺ with 0 dots (all valence electrons lost). Anions: [:Symbol:]ⁿ⁻ with 8 dots (complete octet from gained electrons). Binary ionic compounds show correct ion ratios for charge balance: 1:1 (NaCl: [Na]⁺ [:Cl:]⁻), 1:2 (MgCl₂: [Mg]²⁺ 2[:Cl:]⁻), 2:1 (Na₂O: 2[Na]⁺ [:O:]²⁻), 2:3 (Al₂O₃: 2[Al]³⁺ 3[:O:]²⁻). Polyatomic ions (NH₄⁺, NO₃⁻, SO₄²⁻, OH⁻, etc.) have covalent bonds within the ion (lines between atoms) but ionic bonds with counter-ions; parentheses used when multiple polyatomic ions needed (Ca(NO₃)₂). Lewis structures show empirical formula ratio, not actual crystal ion count; actual ionic compounds exist as 3D crystal lattices with coordination numbers (NaCl: 6:6 coordination). Common errors to avoid: missing brackets, charges inside brackets, cations with dots, anions without complete octets, incorrect ion ratios, drawing covalent bonds between ions. Verification checklist: brackets around each ion ✓, charges outside brackets ✓, cations 0 dots ✓, anions 8 dots ✓, total charge = 0 ✓. Understanding ionic Lewis structures enables prediction of compound formulas, visualization of electron transfer, and foundation for understanding crystal lattice structures and lattice energy.
